evidence_quotes.quote: the absence of protections for games being killed by their publishers

paragraphs.text: At the moment, not only is the main issue of the initiative the absence of protections for games being killed by their publishers; thus breaking EU consumer protections, but also, the absence of legitimiate action to protect the consumer's and the citizen's rights to purchase legal adult material. As highligted in the article linked below, at this moment large american payment processors are making moral decisions on what free people are "allowed" to purchase. Though their decision, on the surface, is merely whether they, as private businesses, will do business related to legal purchases of legal, adult material, due to their power they are indirectly ruling to the legality of legal material, by making it impossible to be purchased by legal means. These payment processors are bypassing governments, and political actors, to introduce unwanted censorship to online spaces. As the second article I've linked here demonstrates, this process, started by activists and maintained by the payment processors, is not concerned whether what they demand is legal. The digital fairness act should include protections for legal art, including not just videogames, but books, other types of games, writing, and visual artforms, from malicious actors wanting to restrict freedom of speech online, worldwide. It should also include regulation and restrictions on large payment processors (for example Visa and Mastercard), from being able to restrict what kind of art is allowed to be sold. Within it's aims and and values, European union publically stands for social justice, liberty and freedom. These recent actions by Mastercard and Visa, as well as from other groups calling for such actions, are in direct contradiction with not only EU law, but EU's values as well, and an action against the freedom of art, which is enshrined in article 13 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights. 1st article: https://www.polygon.com/steam-visa-mastercard-paypal-censorship-project-2025-early-access/?link_source=ta_bluesky_link&taid=68b737bf7f317b0001e4a537&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_medium=social&utm_source=bluesky 2nd article: https://www.thegamer.com/collective-shout-details-it-seeks-removal-of-games-whether-legal-or-not/
